source.title: Did I just do that? Six-month-olds learn the contingency between their vocalizations and a visual reward in 5 minutes

confidence_rationale: Peer-reviewed empirical study, well-powered (experimental vs control design), published in the leading infancy journal. Key finding -- 83% of 6-month-olds increased vocal rate when vocalizations were contingently rewarded with visual feedback -- directly evidences the infant capacity for rapid accidental discovery of action-outcome contingencies. This is the mechanism MECH-189 and INV-073 depend on: accidental actions (first vocalizations, first kicks) discover beneficial contingencies, and the resulting association seeds goal-directed repetition. Confidence capped at 0.73: the study does not document the positive affect/valence change upon discovery, only the behavioral change, leaving the 'accidental benefit contact seeds z_goal' part of MECH-189 supported by inference rather than direct measurement.
failure_signatures: The study uses a non-social visual reward (colorful shapes on iPad), showing the effect does not require social/caregiver contingency. This is relevant to REE's non-social training substrate design but also means we cannot assume affect-amplified discovery from this data alone.

No measurement of affective state change upon discovery: the study tracks vocalization rate only. The internal state change (motivation, wanting, goal formation) upon contingency detection is inferred, not measured.
The contingency is single-action-to-single-outcome: REE needs multi-action affordance repertoires, which the mobile/iPad paradigm cannot straightforwardly address.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Six-month-old infants can detect and learn the contingency between their own vocal actions and a non-social visual reward within five minutes. 83% of infants increased vocalization frequency when doing so produced visual consequences, compared to 47% in controls. Learning proceeds from accidental production of the outcome to systematic increase in the producing action.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-189 posits that high-salience benefit contacts are written to persistent ContextMemory as super-ordinal goal anchors. Keren-Portnoy et al. provide behavioral evidence for the precursor process: an accidental action (vocalization, kick) encounters a benefit contact (moving mobile, colorful display), and the infant rapidly increases the rate of that action. In REE terms, this is the moment when a neutral action-vector in the hippocampal trajectory store gets tagged with a VALENCE_WANTING entry -- before any deliberate goal pursuit, accidental contact bootstraps goal representation. INV-073 (bootstrapping epoch before RL narrowing) is confirmed: infants in the contingent condition are effectively running their own self-generated exploration trial, not responding to externally imposed reward signals.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper does not measure z_goal-equivalent representations or valence tagging. The transition from 'accidentally discovered contingency' to 'persistently maintained goal' requires additional theory (MECH-189) that the paper cannot directly test. The 5-minute learning window is far faster than REE's grid-world exposure timescales, but the principle generalizes.
mapping.source_context: Human infants at 6 months (N reported as experimental vs control groups), laboratory setting, non-social visual reward paradigm. Generalizes the mobile paradigm (Rovee-Collier 1969) to vocal actions without social cues.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality good: peer-reviewed, controlled design, Infancy journal. Mapping fidelity moderate: behavioral mechanism (accidental action -> discovered contingency -> increased rate) maps well; affective/valence consequences are inferred. Transfer risk moderate: infant vocal contingency to REE grid-world benefit contact is indirect but the principle (accidental contact seeds directed repetition) is domain-general.
